topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a monotonous soundtrack to my growing internal turmoil. I stared at the spreadsheet, the numbers mocking me. Sarah, the new intern, had just casually mentioned her salary during a lunch break—a figure that dwarfed mine by a noticeable margin. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, a physical clench that made my hands tremble slightly as I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug. I quickly minimized the window, focusing intensely on a misplaced comma, anything to avoid meeting her eyes again. When she finally looked up, a fleeting glance, I felt my cheeks flush a deep, uncomfortable red. I mumbled an excuse about needing to check inventory, my voice coming out thin and reedy. The silence that followed felt heavy, suffocating, as if the air itself had thickened with unspoken comparison.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over at Mark’s desk, where he was confidently discussing a recent project with the senior management. His easy laughter, the way he spoke about his compensation package—it all felt like a spotlight aimed squarely at my inadequacy. Every time a notification pinged on my screen showing a pay stub, a wave of nausea washed over me. I started avoiding the breakroom entirely, retreating to my cubicle like a hermit. When my manager called me in for a one-on-one, my palms were slick against the keyboard. I managed a strained smile, my gaze fixed on the floor tiles, the words catching in my throat. "Everything is fine," I managed, the sound barely a whisper. Inside, a frantic voice screamed that I was failing, that I was somehow less valuable than the person across the aisle.

[story 3]

The realization hit me during a routine system update. The figures appeared, stark and undeniable, laid bare for my scrutiny. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped my lips. I felt a distinct pressure building behind my eyes, a tightening in my chest that made every breath feel shallow. I spent the next hour meticulously re-reading the data, searching for some hidden error, some logical flaw that would explain the discrepancy. When I finally looked up, my eyes felt watery and unfocused. I couldn't meet anyone's gaze. I quickly packed my bag, the movement jerky and hurried. Walking toward the elevator, I kept my head down, focusing solely on the polished metal doors, trying desperately to shrink myself into the beige wall of the hallway.
